one_liner: A 34-year-old man who emigrated from India 6 months ago presents with a 6-week history of productive cough, now with blood-streaked sputum, drenching night sweats, and a 12-lb unintentional weight loss.

stage2.hpi_extension: His cough is now productive of small amounts of hemoptysis. He reports low-grade fevers daily, drenching night sweats soaking his sheets, anorexia, and fatigue. He lives in a crowded apartment with several family members, one of whom also has a chronic cough. He denies prior TB exposure or treatment. He has had no HIV test. He denies IV drug use or incarceration.
stage2.pmh: No significant medical history; no known prior PPD testing
stage2.social_history: Immigrated from India 6 months ago. Works in a restaurant kitchen. Lives with 5 family members in a small apartment. Non-smoker, no alcohol.
stage2.physical_exam: General: thin, mildly ill-appearing man. HEENT: oral mucosa normal, no thrush. Neck: no lymphadenopathy. Pulmonary: **post-tussive rales and bronchial breath sounds over the right upper lobe**, with dullness to percussion. Cardiac: regular, no murmurs. Abdomen: soft, non-tender, no hepatosplenomegaly. Skin: no rashes; **BCG vaccination scar on left deltoid**.

lab_results.result: Chest X-ray: **cavitary lesion in the right upper lobe apex** with surrounding consolidation and hilar lymphadenopathy. **No calcified Ghon complex** (suggesting reactivation rather than primary TB). High-resolution CT confirms cavitation and **tree-in-bud opacities** indicating endobronchial spread.
lab_results.clinical_significance: Apical cavitation with tree-in-bud nodules is the classic imaging pattern of reactivation TB. Primary TB typically shows a Ghon complex (calcified peripheral granuloma + ipsilateral hilar lymph node).

lab_results.result: Sputum **acid-fast bacilli (AFB) smear positive on all 3 samples** (Ziehl-Neelsen stain). Sputum **NAAT (nucleic acid amplification) positive for Mycobacterium tuberculosis**. Culture on Löwenstein-Jensen medium pending — typically takes 3–6 weeks to grow.
lab_results.clinical_significance: AFB smear and NAAT confirm pulmonary TB. Culture on Löwenstein-Jensen is gold standard but slow; NAAT allows rapid confirmation and early initiation of treatment with drug susceptibility data.

lab_results.result: **Interferon-gamma release assay (IGRA / QuantiFERON-TB Gold) positive**. Tuberculin skin test (PPD) 18 mm induration at 48 hours (**positive**).
lab_results.clinical_significance: IGRA detects interferon-gamma released by T cells exposed to TB-specific antigens and is preferred over PPD in BCG-vaccinated patients because it does not cross-react. Together with positive NAAT, confirms active TB infection.

stage5.final_clue: He is placed on airborne precautions in a negative-pressure room and started on RIPE therapy (rifampin, isoniazid, pyrazinamide, ethambutol) plus pyridoxine; HIV testing returns negative and his household contacts are screened with IGRA.

debrief.classic_presentation: TB classically presents with chronic cough (>3 weeks), hemoptysis, drenching night sweats, fevers, weight loss, and fatigue ('consumption'). Risk factors include immigration from endemic areas (India, Sub-Saharan Africa, SE Asia), HIV infection, homelessness, incarceration, and healthcare work. Primary TB is often asymptomatic; reactivation TB (post-primary) affects the upper lobes where oxygen tension is highest.
debrief.pathophysiology: Mycobacterium tuberculosis is an **acid-fast, aerobic bacillus** with a waxy mycolic-acid-rich cell wall. Inhaled droplets reach alveoli where they are phagocytosed by macrophages but resist killing due to sulfatides and cord factor (trehalose dimycolate). Th1 cells activate macrophages via IFN-γ, forming **caseating granulomas with Langhans giant cells and central caseous necrosis**. Primary TB localizes to the lower lobes (Ghon focus) plus hilar nodes (Ghon complex). Reactivation occurs in the upper-lobe apices due to high oxygen tension. Miliary TB = hematogenous dissemination with millet-seed nodules throughout organs.
debrief.treatment_overview: Active TB: **RIPE therapy** — Rifampin (orange body fluids, CYP450 inducer), Isoniazid (peripheral neuropathy — give with B6/pyridoxine; hepatotoxic), Pyrazinamide (hepatotoxic, hyperuricemia), Ethambutol (optic neuritis, red-green color blindness). 2 months of RIPE, then 4 months of RI. Latent TB (positive PPD/IGRA, normal CXR, asymptomatic): isoniazid × 9 months, or rifampin × 4 months. Directly observed therapy (DOT) improves adherence.
high_yield_pearls: Ghon complex = Ghon focus (calcified lower-lobe granuloma) + ipsilateral hilar lymph node calcification. Ranke complex = fibrosed/calcified Ghon complex after healing.
Reactivation TB favors the upper lobe apices (highest O2 tension) — cavitary lesions are classic.
Caseating granulomas with Langhans giant cells (peripheral horseshoe nuclei) are the pathologic hallmark.
IGRA (QuantiFERON) is preferred in BCG-vaccinated patients — no cross-reactivity because it uses TB-specific antigens (ESAT-6, CFP-10).
RIPE side effects: R = orange body fluids, CYP inducer; I = peripheral neuropathy (give B6), hepatotoxic, SLE-like; P = hyperuricemia/gout, hepatotoxic; E = optic neuritis.
sources: First Aid for the USMLE Step 1 (2017) — Microbiology chapter, 'Mycobacteria — Mycobacterium tuberculosis' section.
First Aid for the USMLE Step 1 (2017) — Respiratory chapter, 'Pneumonia — Granulomatous diseases' section.
